critique.summary: The article describes a comprehensive, regularly updated dataset containing the complete archive of Hacker News posts and comments from 2006 to March 2026. The dataset is stored in Parquet format, updated every 5 minutes, and is accessible via Hugging Face with tools like DuckDB and the datasets library for easy querying and analysis.
mainPoints: The dataset includes every Hacker News item since October 2006, totaling over 47 million items and 11.6 GB of data.
Data is updated live every 5 minutes via the Hacker News Firebase API and monthly authoritative files are refreshed from a ClickHouse mirror.
The dataset is organized by month and includes metadata such as item type, author, score, and timestamps.
It supports various use cases including language model training, sentiment analysis, and research on online community dynamics.
The dataset preserves all content as it appears publicly on Hacker News, including deleted and flagged items, and is released under the Open Data Commons Attribution License.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are accurate and verifiable. Hacker News is a well-known platform operated by Y Combinator, and the dataset described is hosted on Hugging Face, a reputable platform for datasets. The use of the ClickHouse Playground as a source for Hacker News data is consistent with publicly available information. The technical details about data format, update frequency, and usage instructions align with standard practices for large-scale data archiving and analysis. No unsupported or false claims were identified.
considerations: The dataset includes usernames and user-generated content without additional anonymization, which may raise privacy concerns despite being publicly available data.
The Hacker News community has known demographic and topical biases that may affect analyses using this dataset.
Scores reflect snapshots at the time of data sync and may not represent final or current values.
The dataset does not include user profile information such as karma or biographies.
HTML formatting in text fields may require preprocessing for certain types of analysis.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Hacker News data archive in Parquet format, focusing on data size, compression efficiency, update frequency, licensing concerns, and data handling specifics such as deletion and moderation. Users also question technical choices and legal implications related to the dataset.

comments.author: gkbrk
comments.text: My Hacker News items table in ClickHouse has 47,428,860 items, and it's 5.82 GB compressed and 18.18 GB uncompressed. What makes Parquet compression worse here, when both formats are columnar?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid technical question about compression differences between ClickHouse and Parquet, showing understanding of data formats and prompting useful discussion.

comments.author: palmotea
comments.text:
> At midnight UTC, the entire current month is refetched from the source as a single authoritative Parquet file, and today's individual 5-minute blocks are removed from the today/ directory.

Wouldn't that lose deleted/moderated comments?
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully questions the data update strategy and its implications on data integrity, particularly regarding deleted or moderated content.

comments.author: alstonite
comments.text: What happened between 2023 and 2024 to cause the usage dropoff?
comments.analysis: The comment asks a relevant question about a noticeable trend in data usage, though it lacks context or evidence within the thread.

comments.author: Onavo
comments.text: Is is possible to only download a subset? e.g. Show HNs or HN Whoishiring. The Show HNs and HN Whoishiring are very useful for classroom data science i.e. a very useful set of data for students to learn the basic of data cleaning and engineering.
comments.analysis: This comment constructively suggests a practical use case and asks about data subset availability, reflecting a user-focused perspective.

comments.author: 0cf8612b2e1e
comments.text:
Under the Known Limitations section

  deleted and dead are integers. They are stored as 0/1 rather than booleans.
Is there a technical reason to do this? You have the type right there.
comments.analysis: This comment critically examines a technical design choice, suggesting a potential improvement or questioning the rationale.

comments.author: GeoAtreides
comments.text:
is the legal page a placeholder, do words have no meaning?

https://www.ycombinator.com/legal/

Mods, enforce your license terms, you're playing fast and loose with the law (GDPR/CPRA)
comments.analysis: The comment expresses frustration about legal compliance and licensing enforcement, raising important but somewhat confrontational concerns.

comments.author: bstsb
comments.text: what’s the license? “do whatever the fuck you want with the data as long as you don’t get caught”? or does that only work for massive corporations
comments.analysis: This comment uses strong language to question the data licensing terms, reflecting skepticism and frustration but lacking constructive detail.

comments.author: xnx
comments.text: The best source for this data used to be Clickhouse (https://play.clickhouse.com/play?user=play#U0VMRUNUIG1heCh0a...), but it hasn't updated since 2025-12-26.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a factual update about the data source status, useful for context though brief.

comments.author: mlhpdx
comments.text:
Static web content and dynamic data?

> The archive currently spans from 2006-10 to 2026-03-16 23:55 UTC, with 47,358,772 items committed.

That’s more than 5 minutes ago by a day or two. No big deal, but a little bit depressing this is still how we do things in 2026.
comments.analysis: This comment critiques the data update latency with a mix of factual observation and mild disappointment, providing some context but limited technical insight.
